citations.narrative:
The facility failed to handle a bird with the necessary care to prevent injury. On March 25, 2026, a male lorikeet
(#225011), was being transferred between holding areas when the door was not closed quickly enough, resulting in the
bird becoming entrapped and subsequently dying.
Handling errors can have severe or fatal consequences for birds due to their unique anatomy and behavioral responses.
Because birds often panic when startled, even a brief lapse in handling technique can escalate into a life threatening
incident. For these reasons, consistent, deliberate, and well coordinated handling practices are critical to ensuring their
safety and welfare.
Handling of all animals shall be done as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not cause trauma,
overheating, excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort. Correct this day, May 29,
2026, forward.

The facility failed to secure a Rosybill duck in its’ enclosure. On November 12, 2025, a Rosybill duck (#203189) was
reported missing from its’ primary enclosure. No signs of a struggle were found. No feathers were found. There was no
evidence of predators breeching the enclosure fence. The wading pool was drained and there was no evidence of a
carcass or feathers. There is no public interaction with the animals. The public observes the exhibit from a suspended
bridge approximately 20ft high. The whereabouts of the duck could not be determined by the facility.
Enclosures must be constructed to contain the animals within and prevent predators from entering. Birds that escape their
enclosures may be restricted in their access to food, water, and shelter, and are at increased risk of predation and injury.
Primary enclosures must be designed and constructed of suitable materials so that they are structurally sound. The
primary enclosures must be kept in good repair. Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that they
contain the birds securely. Correct this day, May 29, 2026, forward.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representative.
n

narrative:
2.131(b)(1)  Critical
   
Handling of animals. 
The facility failed to handle a bird with the necessary care to prevent injury. On March 25, 2026, a male lorikeet 
(#225011), was being transferred between holding areas when the door was not closed quickly enough, resulting in the 
bird becoming entrapped and subsequently dying. 
 
Handling errors can have severe or fatal consequences for birds due to their unique anatomy and behavioral responses. 
Because birds often panic when startled, even a brief lapse in handling technique can escalate into a life threatening 
incident. For these reasons, consistent, deliberate, and well coordinated handling practices are critical to ensuring their 
safety and welfare. 
 
Handling of all animals shall be done as expeditiously and carefully as possible in a manner that does not cause trauma, 
overheating, excessive cooling, behavioral stress, physical harm, or unnecessary discomfort. Correct this day, May 29, 
2026, forward. 
3.153(a)(1)(iii)  Critical
   
Primary enclosures. 
The facility failed to secure a Rosybill duck in its’ enclosure. On November 12, 2025, a Rosybill duck (#203189) was 
reported missing from its’ primary enclosure. No signs of a struggle were found. No feathers were found. There was no 
evidence of predators breeching the enclosure fence. The wading pool was drained and there was no evidence of a 
carcass or feathers. There is no public interaction with the animals. The public observes the exhibit from a suspended 
bridge approximately 20ft high. The whereabouts of the duck could not be determined by the facility. 
 
Enclosures must be constructed to contain the animals within and prevent predators from entering. Birds that escape their 
enclosures may be restricted in their access to food, water, and shelter, and are at increased risk of predation and injury. 
 
Primary enclosures must be designed and constructed of suitable materials so that they are structurally sound. The 
primary enclosures must be kept in good repair. Primary enclosures must be constructed and maintained so that they 
contain the birds securely. Correct this day, May 29, 2026, forward.

This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representative.
